Fern Polrrrcer- PRACTrces C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 c 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June 5,2002
Ms. Harriet A. Steiner
McDonough, Holland & Allen
555 Capitol Mall, 9th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-02-095
Dear Ms. Steiner:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
FACTS
You serve as general counsel to the Sacramento Metropolitan Cable Television
Commission (the "Cable Commission"), a joint powers agency that administers cable
franchises and licenses. On behalf of the Cable Commission, you are seeking written
advice from the FPPC on the application of $ 84308 to the facts as set forth below.2
The Cable Commission is a joint powers agency whose board is composed of
elected officials from the Sacramento County Board of Supervisors and city councils
from cities located in Sacramento County. The Cable Commission issues cable television
franchises for the County of Sacramento and the cities within Sacramento County (except
Isleton), regulates the cable franchisees, and enforces the requirements of the cable
franchise. The Cable Franchise Ordinance requires Cable Commission approval of any
transfer of the cable franchise. Transfers include, but are not limited to, sales and
changes in the control of entities that have ownership and control of the franchisees.
' Goremment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 This letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 2
Approval of the transfer or change of control lies within the discretion of the Cable
Commission, subject to the rules set forth in the Cable Franchise Ordinance.
Three wholly-owned subsidiaries of AT&T Broadband jointly hold the
Sacramento cable franchise and are the cable franchisees. AT&T Broadband guarantees
performance of the franchise through a corporate guarantee. AT&T Corp. is the publicly-
traded parent corporation of AT&T Broadband.
On February 27,2002, the Cable Commission staff received an application to
approve a charrge in control of the franchisees. This application was based on a proposed
merger between AT&T and Comcast. The application includes corporate organization
charts that describe the proposed merger. On February 27,2002, the staff notified the
Cable Commission members by means of a short memo, that the application had been
received. On March 28,2002, commissioners received an agenda for their April 4,2002
meeting, which included a public hearing on the AT&T - Comcast merger.
On April 4,2002, the Cable Commission held its first public hearing on the
merger. The Cable Commission's own regulations require that applicants and others who
wish to testifr before the Cable Commission disclose whether they have made any
campaign contributions to Cable Commission members or certiff that they have not made
any such contributions, as part of their sign-up card to speak or testify at the Cable
Commission meeting.
At the April4 meeting, AT&T representatives handed the Cable Commission
secretary a letter at the same time as they handed in their speaker cards. A copy of this
letter (unread until April 5) disclosed that AT&T and the public relations firm of Runyon
Saltzman had made certain campaign contributions to three commissioners - Sacramento
County Supervisor Illa Collin, Sacramento City Councilmember Robbie Waters and
Sacramento County Supervisor Roger Dickinson. All of these commissioners ran for
reelection in the March 2002pimary. AT&T Broadband and Runyon Saltzman made
these contributions to the commissioners separately.
Runyon Saltzman has been on retainer by AT&T since AT&T acquired the cable
franchise about 18 months ago. Runyon Saltzman has been the public relations firm for
the prior owners of the cable franchise and has done public relations work on behalf of
the cable franchisees for many years.
The specific facts regarding the campaign contributions, as set forth in the AT&T
April 4th letter, are as follows: Illa Collin received $249 in campaign contributions from
Runyon Saltzman on June 5,2001 and$249 from AT&T Broadband on July 9,2001.
Robbie Waters received $249 in campaign contributions from AT&T Broadband on
September 25,2001, and Runyon Saltzman contributed $249 to him on the same date.
Roger Dickinson received $500 in campaign contributions from Runyon Saltzman on
Aprll27,200l.


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 3
Even though you are not sure whether the thirty-day window for return of
contributions under $ 84308 still applies, you have advised these three commissioners to
return campaign contributions to AT&T andlor Runyon Saltzman so that their aggregated
contributions do not exceed $250. The Cable Commission is scheduled to hear the
proposed merger application on June 6,2002.
ANALYSIS
Section 84308 disqualifies any "officer" of apublic agency, who is running or has
run for elective office, from participating in decisions affecting his or her campaign
contributors. The law disqualifies the officer from participating in certain proceedings if
the official has received campaign contributions of more than $250 from a party,
participant or their agents within the 12 months preceding the decision. It also requires
disclosure on the record of the proceeding of all campaign contributions received from
these persons during that period. The members of the Cable Commission are officers
subject to the provisions of $ 84308. ($ 84308(aX4); Reg. 18438.1(a) and (b).)
Section 84308(c) provides as follows:
". . . No officer of an agency shall make, participate in making, or in any
way attempt to use his or her official position to influence the decision in a
proceedins involvins a license. permit. or other entitlement for use
pending before the agency if the officer has willfully or knowingly
received a contribution in an amount of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) within the preceding 12 months from a party or his or her
agent ...." (Emphasis added.)
1. Does the Cable Commission's consideration of the proposed change in
control of the cable franchise occasioned by the proposed AT&T - Comcast merger
constitute a proceeding for purposes of$ 84308?
Section 84308's definition of a proceeding involving a "license, permit, or other
entitlement for use" includes franchises, as set forth in subdivision (aX5):
"(a) The definitions set forth in this subdivision shall govern the
interpretation of this section.
11. . .fl
(5) 'License, permit, or other entitlement for use' means all business,
professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other
entitlements for use, including all entitlements for land use, all contracts
(other than competitively bid, labor, or personal employment contracts),
and all franchises."
In addition, under Reg. 18438.2 aproceeding involving a "license, permit, or
other entitlement for use" includes modifications to franchises. Reg. 1,8438.2 provides as
follows:


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 4
"(a) For purposes of Government Code Section 84308, a 'proceeding
involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use' includes any
proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modify a license, permit or
other entitlement for use.
(b) A proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use
is 'pending before' an agency:
(1) When the application has been filed, the proceeding has been
commenced, or the issue has otherwise been submitted to the jurisdiction
of an agency for its determination or other action;
(2) It is the type of proceeding where the officers of the agency are
required by law to make a decision, or the matter has been otherwise
submitted to the officers of the agency for their decision; and
(3) The decision of the officer or officers with respect to the proceeding
will not be purely ministerial."
You ask whether the need for approval of the change in control of the franchise
based'on the merger of the parent of the existing franchisees with another cable company
constitutes a grant, restriction or modification of an entitlement for use. You state that
under the proposed merger, (1) the franchisees would not change; (2) the guarantor will
change from AT&T Broadband, LLC to AT&T Broadband Co.p.; (3) the parent and
controlling corporation will change from AT&T Corp.to AT&T Comcast; and (4) the
current controlling owner of Comcast will have control of the new merged corporation.
Approval of the transfer of control application is a major matter that has been
considered and negotiated by the Cable Commission for several months. Cable
Commissioners were notified about the proposed AT&T - Comcast merger as soon as the
application was received in February. In addition, the Cable Commission held a public
hearing to consider the transfer of the franchise and licenses held by AT&T Broadband to
AT&T Comcast Corporation on April 4,2002. The item is on the Cable Commission's
June 6, 2002 meeting agenda.
For purposes of $ 84308, the Cable Commission's consideration of the AT&T -
Comcast mergerltransfer of control application is considered a proceeding. This
conclusion, however, is strictly limited to $ 84308 of the Act and has no relevance or
bearing on the categoization of the transaction for purposes of federal, state or local law
regulating cable transfers.
2. N4ay a public relations firm such as Runyon Saltzman be an agent for
purposes of $ 84308? If so, what triggers the agent relationship? Would Runyon
Saltzman be an agent if AT&T directs Runyon Saltzman to stop working on the
merger, even though it may continue to use them for its public relations needs?
With respect to "agents" under $ 84308, Reg. 18438.3 provides as follows


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 5
"(a) For purposes of Government Code Section 84308, a person is the
'agerrt' of aparty to, or a participant in, a proceeding involving a license,
permit or other entitlement for use only if he or she represents that person
in corurection with the proceeding involving the license. permit or other
entitlement for use. If an individual acting as an agent is also acting as an
employee or member of a law, architectural, engineering or consulting
firm, or a similar entity or corporation, both the entity or corporation and
the individual are 'agents."' (Emphasis added.)
Under $ 84308, Runyon Saltzman is an agent of AT&T if Runyon Saltzman
represents AT&T in connection with the merger/transfer of control application.
You state that you could not locate any specific authority to quantify a specific
trigger or action for when a person becomes an agent. You note that"la) person could do
any of a variety of activities on behalf of aparty - anything from calling a
Commissioner(s), meeting a Commissioner(s), writing a letter to the Commission, or
testifying at a public hearing. All of these activities would be done on behalf of the party.
Many, if not all of these activities could be for the purpose of influencing the member's
vote on the proceeding, but do they all constitute representation 'in connection with the
proceeding'?" All of these activities, if done on behalf of AT&T for the purpose of
influencing a commissioner's vote on the proceeding would constitute representation in
connection with the proceeding for purposes of $ 84308.
With respect to the current merger/transfer of control application, you state that
representatives of AT&T testified at the April4, 2002 public hearing. No one from
Runyon Saltzman testified about the merger during the April 4,2002 hearing, though
they attended the hearing.
In your request for advice, you summarizedyour understanding of the relationship
between Runyon Saltzman and AT&T as follows:
"Runyon, Saltzman has been on retainer by AT&T since AT&T
acquired the cable franchise about 18 months ago. Runyon Saltzman has
been the public relations firm for the prior owners of the cable franchise
and has done public relations work on behalf of the cable franchisees for
many years. The firm produces advertisements, arranges meetings and
does some local advocacy work for AT&T. During the prior transfer
process when AT&T acquired the Sacramento franchise from Comcast
(about 18 months ago), Runyon Saltzman arranged meetings with
Commissioners and AT&T and Comcast representatives and participated
in those meetings. The purpose of those meetings was to influence the
Commissioners to approve the sale of the Sacramento franchise from
Comcast to AT&T."
In addition, pursuant to our telephone conversation of May 28,2002 it appears
that Runyon Saltzman is currently under contract with AT&T to, among other things,


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 6
represent AT&T in connection with the AT&T - Comcast transfer of control application.
Runyon Saltzman has been on retainer with AT&T for ayear and ahalf, worked on the
previous transfer of control application, and is under contract to work on the current one.
These facts are sufficient to establish an agerLcy relationship between Runyon Saltzman
and AT&T for purposes of $ 84308 and this proceeding.
An agdncy relationship can clearly be terminated if an agent is no longer
representinE aparty in connection with a proceeding. Similarly, if apublic relations firm
was hired by aparty for matters totally unrelated to a pending proceeding, it would not
consitute an agency relationship for purposes of $ 84308. However, aparty cannot
sidestep the aggregation provisions of $ 84308 by hiring an agent to work on a
proceeding and then terminate the agent or direct the agent not to work on the proceeding
immediately prior to the vote. For the party to claim that their contributions and those of
their agent are not aggregated in this circumstance would be circumvention of the
disqualification provisions of $ 84308.
3. For purposes of aggregating contributions, which dates do you use in
aggregating the contributions when the contributions by the party and the agent
were given on different days?
With respect to aggregation of contributions, Reg. 18438.3(b) states
"(b) To determine whether a contribution of more than $250 has been
made by a person or his or her agent, contributions made by that person
within the preceding 1.2 months shall be aggregated with those made by
his or her agent within the preceding 12 months or the period of the
agency relationship, whichever is shorter."
For this question you are only concerned with Commissioner Collin who received
a contribution of $249 from Runyon Saltzman on June 5,2001, and a contribution of
$249 from AT&T on July 9,200L The upcoming Cable Commission meeting will be
held on June 6, 2002. Applying Reg. 18438.3(b) as of June 6,2002, contributions made
by AT&T within the preceding 12 months (the contribution of $249 made on July 9,
2001) are aggregated with those made by Runyon Saltzman within the precedingT2
months ($0, since the most recent contribution to Commissioner Collin from Runyon
Saltzman was made on June 5, 2001), for a total of $249. Assuming there are no further
contributions between now and September 6 (three months after the scheduled
consideration) from AT&T or Runyon Saltzman, Commissioner Collin will have
received a total of $249 from AT&T and Runyon Saltzman and will not be disqualified
under $ 84308 from participating in the June 6, 2002 meeting.
4. Return of contributions under $ 84308. When did the Comissioners have
knowledge of the proceeding and the contributions?
If an officer has received a contribution totaling more than $250 or more from a
party or their agent in a proceeding during the preceding 12 months, the officer is


File No. A-02-095
Page No. 7
disqualified from participating in the decision on the proceeding. However, the officer
may return the contribution or that portion of the contribution over $249 andparticipate
in the proceeding. This option is available to the officer only for 30 days after he or she
has knowledge of both the pending proceeding and the contribution. ($ 8a308(c).) (See,
e.g. LaPorte Advice Letter, No. I-93-31 5 and Pleines Advice Letter, No. A-87-220.)
Reg. 18438.7 specifies when an officer knows or should have known about a
pending proceeding and a contribution. Reg. L8438.7 states that for purposes of $ 84308:
"(b) An officer knows, or should have known, about a proceeding
pending before the agency if either:
(1) The officer has received notice of the license, permit or other
entitlement proceeding. Notice includes receipt of an agenda or docket
identifying the proceeding and the party or other persons affected by
name; or
(2) the officer has actual knowledge of the proceeding.
(c) An officer knows, or should have known about a contribution if:
(1) The contribution has been disclosed by the party pursuant to Section
84308(d); or
(2) The officer has actual knowledge of the contribution."
As to the commissioner's knowledge of the proceeding, you state that
the initial transfer of control application was received by Commission staff on February
27 , 2002, and the commissioners were notified of the filing that same day by means of a
short memo. The commissioners received their agendas for the Apil 4,2002 meeting on
the transfer application on approximately March 28,2002, and the first public hearing
was held on the matter on April 4,2002. You ask which of these dates constitutes notice
to commissioners. Under these facts, February 27 ,2002 is considered the date the
commissioners knew about the proceeding.
Reg. 18438.7(c) states that an officer knows or should have known about a
contribution if the contribution has been disclosed by aparty or the officer has actual
knowledge of the contribution. Under the facts presented, the commissioners had
knowledge of the contributions and the proceeding on the filing of the Aprll 4,2002letter
from AT&T listing the contributions from AT&T and Runyon Saltzman to the three
commissioners. Any contributions in excess of $250 would have had to be returned by
May 4,2002by Commissioners Dickinson and Waters to enable them to participate in
the June 6 decision.
In addition, note that $ 84308 prohibits an officer from accepting, soliciting or
directing a contribution over $250 from aparty to a proceeding for three months after the
final decision. The statutory prohibition applies to any officer of the agency before
which the proceeding was pending, and there is no exception made for an officer who
recused himself or herself from the proceeding. (Boznanskt Advice Letter, No. A-00-
172.)


File No. 4-02-095
Page No. 8
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
-N-/. P, tJ ayr'*
By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
HPWjg

, however, is strictly limited to $ 84308 of the Act and has no relevance or
bearing on the categoization of the transaction for purposes of federal, state or local law
regulating cable transfers.

immediately prior to the vote. For the party to claim that their contributions and those of
their agent are not aggregated in this circumstance would be circumvention of the
disqualification provisions of $ 84308.
3. For purposes of aggregating contributions, which dates do you use in
aggregating the contributions when the contributions by the party and the agent
were given on different days?
With respect to aggregation of contributions, Reg. 18438.3(b) states
"(b) To determine whether a contribution of more than $250 has been
made by a person or his or her agent, contributions made by that person
within the preceding 1.2 months shall be aggregated with those made by
his or her agent within the preceding 12 months or the period of the
agency relationship, whichever is shorter."
For this question you are only concerned with Commissioner Collin who received
a contribution of $249 from Runyon Saltzman on June 5,2001, and a contribution of
$249 from AT&T on July 9,200L The upcoming Cable Commission meeting will be
held on June 6, 2002. Applying Reg. 18438.3(b) as of June 6,2002, contributions made
by AT&T within the preceding 12 months (the contribution of $249 made on July 9,

months ($0, since the most recent contribution to Commissioner Collin from Runyon
Saltzman was made on June 5, 2001), for a total of $249. Assuming there are no further
contributions between now and September 6 (three months after the scheduled
consideration) from AT&T or Runyon Saltzman, Commissioner Collin will have

4. Return of contributions under $ 84308. When did the Comissioners have

identifying the proceeding and the party or other persons affected by
name; or
(2) the officer has actual knowledge of the proceeding.

Commission (the "Cable Commission"), a joint powers agency that administers cable

changes in the control of entities that have ownership and control of the franchisees.

franchise about 18 months ago. Runyon Saltzman has been the public relations firm for

Aprll27,200l.

sections.analysis:
Section 84308 disqualifies any "officer" of apublic agency, who is running or has
run for elective office, from participating in decisions affecting his or her campaign
contributors. The law disqualifies the officer from participating in certain proceedings if
the official has received campaign contributions of more than $250 from a party,
participant or their agents within the 12 months preceding the decision. It also requires
disclosure on the record of the proceeding of all campaign contributions received from
these persons during that period. The members of the Cable Commission are officers
subject to the provisions of $ 84308. ($ 84308(aX4); Reg. 18438.1(a) and (b).)
Section 84308(c) provides as follows:
". . . No officer of an agency shall make, participate in making, or in any
way attempt to use his or her official position to influence the decision in a
proceedins involvins a license. permit. or other entitlement for use
pending before the agency if the officer has willfully or knowingly
received a contribution in an amount of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) within the preceding 12 months from a party or his or her
agent ...." (Emphasis added.)
1. Does the Cable Commission's consideration of the proposed change in
control of the cable franchise occasioned by the proposed AT&T - Comcast merger
constitute a proceeding for purposes of$ 84308?
Section 84308's definition of a proceeding involving a "license, permit, or other
entitlement for use" includes franchises, as set forth in subdivision (aX5):
"(a) The definitions set forth in this subdivision shall govern the
interpretation of this section.
11. . .fl
(5) 'License, permit, or other entitlement for use' means all business,
professional, trade and land use licenses and permits and all other
entitlements for use, including all entitlements for land use, all contracts
(other than competitively bid, labor, or personal employment contracts),
and all franchises."
In addition, under Reg. 18438.2 aproceeding involving a "license, permit, or
other entitlement for use" includes modifications to franchises. Reg. 1,8438.2 provides as
follows:
"(a) For purposes of Government Code Section 84308, a 'proceeding
involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use' includes any
proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modify a license, permit or
other entitlement for use.
(b) A proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use
is 'pending before' an agency:
(1) When the application has been filed, the proceeding has been
commenced, or the issue has otherwise been submitted to the jurisdiction
of an agency for its determination or other action;
(2) It is the type of proceeding where the officers of the agency are
required by law to make a decision, or the matter has been otherwise
submitted to the officers of the agency for their decision; and
(3) The decision of the officer or officers with respect to the proceeding
will not be purely ministerial."
You ask whether the need for approval of the change in control of the franchise
based'on the merger of the parent of the existing franchisees with another cable company
constitutes a grant, restriction or modification of an entitlement for use. You state that
under the proposed merger, (1) the franchisees would not change; (2) the guarantor will
change from AT&T Broadband, LLC to AT&T Broadband Co.p.; (3) the parent and
controlling corporation will change from AT&T Corp.to AT&T Comcast; and (4) the
current controlling owner of Comcast will have control of the new merged corporation.
Approval of the transfer of control application is a major matter that has been
considered and negotiated by the Cable Commission for several months. Cable
Commissioners were notified about the proposed AT&T - Comcast merger as soon as the
application was received in February. In addition, the Cable Commission held a public
hearing to consider the transfer of the franchise and licenses held by AT&T Broadband to
AT&T Comcast Corporation on April 4,2002. The item is on the Cable Commission's
June 6, 2002 meeting agenda.
For purposes of $ 84308, the Cable Commission's consideration of the AT&T -
Comcast mergerltransfer of control application is considered a proceeding. This

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: , however, is strictly limited to $ 84308 of the Act and has no relevance or
bearing on the categoization of the transaction for purposes of federal, state or local law
regulating cable transfers.
2. N4ay a public relations firm such as Runyon Saltzman be an agent for
purposes of $ 84308? If so, what triggers the agent relationship? Would Runyon
Saltzman be an agent if AT&T directs Runyon Saltzman to stop working on the
merger, even though it may continue to use them for its public relations needs?
With respect to "agents" under $ 84308, Reg. 18438.3 provides as follows
"(a) For purposes of Government Code Section 84308, a person is the
'agerrt' of aparty to, or a participant in, a proceeding involving a license,
permit or other entitlement for use only if he or she represents that person
in corurection with the proceeding involving the license. permit or other
entitlement for use. If an individual acting as an agent is also acting as an
employee or member of a law, architectural, engineering or consulting
firm, or a similar entity or corporation, both the entity or corporation and
the individual are 'agents."' (Emphasis added.)
Under $ 84308, Runyon Saltzman is an agent of AT&T if Runyon Saltzman
represents AT&T in connection with the merger/transfer of control application.
You state that you could not locate any specific authority to quantify a specific
trigger or action for when a person becomes an agent. You note that"la) person could do
any of a variety of activities on behalf of aparty - anything from calling a
Commissioner(s), meeting a Commissioner(s), writing a letter to the Commission, or
testifying at a public hearing. All of these activities would be done on behalf of the party.
Many, if not all of these activities could be for the purpose of influencing the member's
vote on the proceeding, but do they all constitute representation 'in connection with the
proceeding'?" All of these activities, if done on behalf of AT&T for the purpose of
influencing a commissioner's vote on the proceeding would constitute representation in
connection with the proceeding for purposes of $ 84308.
With respect to the current merger/transfer of control application, you state that
representatives of AT&T testified at the April4, 2002 public hearing. No one from
Runyon Saltzman testified about the merger during the April 4,2002 hearing, though
they attended the hearing.
In your request for advice, you summarizedyour understanding of the relationship
between Runyon Saltzman and AT&T as follows:
"Runyon, Saltzman has been on retainer by AT&T since AT&T
acquired the cable franchise about 18 months ago. Runyon Saltzman has
been the public relations firm for the prior owners of the cable franchise
and has done public relations work on behalf of the cable franchisees for
many years. The firm produces advertisements, arranges meetings and
does some local advocacy work for AT&T. During the prior transfer
process when AT&T acquired the Sacramento franchise from Comcast
(about 18 months ago), Runyon Saltzman arranged meetings with
Commissioners and AT&T and Comcast representatives and participated
in those meetings. The purpose of those meetings was to influence the
Commissioners to approve the sale of the Sacramento franchise from
Comcast to AT&T."
In addition, pursuant to our telephone conversation of May 28,2002 it appears
that Runyon Saltzman is currently under contract with AT&T to, among other things,
represent AT&T in connection with the AT&T - Comcast transfer of control application.
Runyon Saltzman has been on retainer with AT&T for ayear and ahalf, worked on the
previous transfer of control application, and is under contract to work on the current one.
These facts are sufficient to establish an agerLcy relationship between Runyon Saltzman
and AT&T for purposes of $ 84308 and this proceeding.
An agdncy relationship can clearly be terminated if an agent is no longer
representinE aparty in connection with a proceeding. Similarly, if apublic relations firm
was hired by aparty for matters totally unrelated to a pending proceeding, it would not
consitute an agency relationship for purposes of $ 84308. However, aparty cannot
sidestep the aggregation provisions of $ 84308 by hiring an agent to work on a
proceeding and then terminate the agent or direct the agent not to work on the proceeding
immediately prior to the vote. For the party to claim that their contributions and those of
their agent are not aggregated in this circumstance would be circumvention of the
disqualification provisions of $ 84308.
3. For purposes of aggregating contributions, which dates do you use in
aggregating the contributions when the contributions by the party and the agent
were given on different days?
With respect to aggregation of contributions, Reg. 18438.3(b) states
"(b) To determine whether a contribution of more than $250 has been
made by a person or his or her agent, contributions made by that person
within the preceding 1.2 months shall be aggregated with those made by
his or her agent within the preceding 12 months or the period of the
agency relationship, whichever is shorter."
For this question you are only concerned with Commissioner Collin who received
a contribution of $249 from Runyon Saltzman on June 5,2001, and a contribution of
$249 from AT&T on July 9,200L The upcoming Cable Commission meeting will be
held on June 6, 2002. Applying Reg. 18438.3(b) as of June 6,2002, contributions made
by AT&T within the preceding 12 months (the contribution of $249 made on July 9,
2001) are aggregated with those made by Runyon Saltzman within the precedingT2
months ($0, since the most recent contribution to Commissioner Collin from Runyon
Saltzman was made on June 5, 2001), for a total of $249. Assuming there are no further
contributions between now and September 6 (three months after the scheduled
consideration) from AT&T or Runyon Saltzman, Commissioner Collin will have
received a total of $249 from AT&T and Runyon Saltzman and will not be disqualified
under $ 84308 from participating in the June 6, 2002 meeting.
4. Return of contributions under $ 84308. When did the Comissioners have
knowledge of the proceeding and the contributions?
If an officer has received a contribution totaling more than $250 or more from a
party or their agent in a proceeding during the preceding 12 months, the officer is
disqualified from participating in the decision on the proceeding. However, the officer
may return the contribution or that portion of the contribution over $249 andparticipate
in the proceeding. This option is available to the officer only for 30 days after he or she
has knowledge of both the pending proceeding and the contribution. ($ 8a308(c).) (See,
e.g. LaPorte Advice Letter, No. I-93-31 5 and Pleines Advice Letter, No. A-87-220.)
Reg. 18438.7 specifies when an officer knows or should have known about a
pending proceeding and a contribution. Reg. L8438.7 states that for purposes of $ 84308:
"(b) An officer knows, or should have known, about a proceeding
pending before the agency if either:
(1) The officer has received notice of the license, permit or other
entitlement proceeding. Notice includes receipt of an agenda or docket
identifying the proceeding and the party or other persons affected by
name; or
(2) the officer has actual knowledge of the proceeding.
(c) An officer knows, or should have known about a contribution if:
(1) The contribution has been disclosed by the party pursuant to Section
84308(d); or
(2) The officer has actual knowledge of the contribution."
As to the commissioner's knowledge of the proceeding, you state that
the initial transfer of control application was received by Commission staff on February
27 , 2002, and the commissioners were notified of the filing that same day by means of a
short memo. The commissioners received their agendas for the Apil 4,2002 meeting on
the transfer application on approximately March 28,2002, and the first public hearing
was held on the matter on April 4,2002. You ask which of these dates constitutes notice
to commissioners. Under these facts, February 27 ,2002 is considered the date the
commissioners knew about the proceeding.
Reg. 18438.7(c) states that an officer knows or should have known about a
contribution if the contribution has been disclosed by aparty or the officer has actual
knowledge of the contribution. Under the facts presented, the commissioners had
knowledge of the contributions and the proceeding on the filing of the Aprll 4,2002letter
from AT&T listing the contributions from AT&T and Runyon Saltzman to the three
commissioners. Any contributions in excess of $250 would have had to be returned by
May 4,2002by Commissioners Dickinson and Waters to enable them to participate in
the June 6 decision.
In addition, note that $ 84308 prohibits an officer from accepting, soliciting or
directing a contribution over $250 from aparty to a proceeding for three months after the
final decision. The statutory prohibition applies to any officer of the agency before
which the proceeding was pending, and there is no exception made for an officer who
recused himself or herself from the proceeding. (Boznanskt Advice Letter, No. A-00-
172.)

Steiner McDonough, Holland & Allen 555 Capitol Mall, 9th Floor Sacramento, CA 95814 Re Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. 4-02-095 Dear Ms. Steiner: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").I FACTS You serve as general counsel to the Sacramento Metropolitan Cable Television Commission (the "Cable Commission"), a joint powers agency that administers cable franchises and licenses. On behalf of the Cable Commission, you are seeking written advice from the FPPC on the application of $ 84308 to the facts as set forth below.2 The Cable Commission is a joint powers agency whose board is composed of elected officials from the Sacramento County Board of Supervisors and city councils from cities located in Sacramento County. The Cable Commission issues cable television franchises for the County of Sacramento and the cities within Sacramento County (except Isleton), regulates the cable franchisees, and enforces the requirements of the cable franchise. The Cable Franchise Ordinance requires Cable Commission approval of any transfer of the cable franchise. Transfers include, but are not limited to, sales and changes in the control of entities that have ownership and control of the franchisees. ' Goremment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 This letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) File No. A-02-095 Page No. 2 Approval of the transfer or change of control lies within the discretion of the Cable Commission, subject to the rules set forth in the Cable Franchise Ordinance. Three wholly-owned subsidiaries of AT&T Broadband jointly hold the Sacramento cable franchise and are the cable franchisees. AT&T Broadband guarantees performance of the franchise through a corporate guarantee. AT&T Corp. is the publicly- traded parent corporation of AT&T Broadband. On February 27,2002, the Cable Commission staff received an application to approve a charrge in control of the franchisees. This application was based on a proposed merger between AT&T and Comcast. The application includes corporate organization charts that describe the proposed merger. On February 27,2002, the staff notified the Cable Commission members by means of a short memo, that the application had been received. On March 28,2002, commissioners received an agenda for their April 4,2002 meeting, which included a public hearing on the AT&T - Comcast merger. On April 4,2002, the Cable Commission held its first public hearing on the merger. The Cable Commission's own regulations require that applicants and others who wish to testifr before the Cable Commission disclose whether they have made any campaign contributions to Cable Commission members or certiff that they have not made any such contributions, as part of their sign-up card to speak
